nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich areas of deep lagoons and seaward reefs, from the lower surge zone down to roughly 50 m; tends to be territorial and is generally observed close to rockwork and coral cover

decorPreferences: large rockwork and caves for retreat

diet.notes: Carnivorous; in the wild has been documented eating echinoderms, molluscs, small fishes, tunicates, sponges, hydrozoans, and some algae (FishBase trophic level ~3.4). In captivity a varied meaty diet of squid, krill, clams, mussels, mysis, and whole hard-shelled shrimp or snails has been widely recommended — the hard items are generally advised to help wear down continuously growing incisor-like teeth. Feeding once or twice daily tends to be sufficient.

goodWith: other large robust predators of similar size

summary: A boldly patterned Indo-Pacific triggerfish with an emerald-green body and yellow-orange stripes. Widely considered one of the most aggressive triggerfishes and is not reef-safe; best suited to large species-only or predator FOWLR systems.
careNotes: Balistapus undulatus has been widely reported to be among the most aggressive triggerfishes in the aquarium trade — sources describe it as territorial and willing to attack tankmates much larger than itself, as well as biting hands, hoses, and even silicone seals or acrylic panels. A species-only or large predator-only tank is generally advised; reef compatibility is broadly considered impossible because it will consume invertebrates, crustaceans, molluscs, and corals, and will rearrange or dislodge rockwork. Tank size: mainstream hobby sources (LiveAquaria, ReefTrak) recommend 180 gal (~680 L) minimum, while one source (AquariumDomain) suggests as low as 75 gal (~284 L); given the species' documented aggression, full adult size around 30 cm, and need for territory plus swimming space, the figure used here (~700 L) biases toward the larger end and rounds to a common aquarium size. Temperature: LiveAquaria/ReefTrak give 72–78°F (~22–26°C) and FishBase wild data spans 25.1–29.1°C; the range used here (23–27°C) averages across these. The species produces audible grunting sounds when stressed or excited. Continuously growing teeth tend to require hard-shelled food items to prevent overgrowth. Caution is generally advised when servicing the tank — bites have been widely reported. Captive breeding has not been documented in reviewed sources; trade specimens are wild-caught.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reported. In the wild, spawning has been described as occurring in loose aggregations with nesting in reef channels; eggs are laid in a single spongy cluster in a shallow excavation, hatching nocturnally, with one or two adults reported to guard the nest. Sexual dimorphism is not well documented in aquarium literature.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page for Undulate Triggerfish (Balistapus undulatus). Provided adult size (12 in), min tank (180 gal), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), diet, temperament, care level, and reef compatibility.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name, taxonomy (Balistidae, Tetraodontiformes), max length 30 cm TL (common 26 cm), depth 0–60 m, wild temperature 25.1–29.1°C, native range across the Indo-Pacific (Red Sea to Tuamoto Is., S. Japan to GBR/New Caledonia), omnivorous benthic diet (trophic level 3.4), and wild reproduction (nesting in channels, eggs in spongy clusters, nest guarding by adults).

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (12 in / ~30 cm), temperature (72–79°F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), and aggression notes. Reported min tank size of 75 gal — notably lower than LiveAquaria's 180 gal; this lower figure was not adopted given the species' documented adult size and aggression.

additional.name: ReefTrak
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12 in), min tank (180 gal), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.3), salinity (1.02–1.025), carnivorous diet, reef-incompatibility, and Indo-Pacific native range. Used as third-source confirmation after the designated Reef2Reef wiki link returned HTTP 403.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reported. Wild observations describe channel-nesting with eggs deposited as a spongy cluster in a shallow excavation, hatched nocturnally, with nest-guarding by one or two adults.
